questions.stem: This figure contrasts normal cells with cells in which lysosomal enzymes fail to reach the lysosome and are instead secreted, due to loss of a phosphotransferase. Which disorder does the right panel represent?

explanation.summary: Deficiency of GlcNAc-1-phosphotransferase prevents mannose-6-phosphate tagging of lysosomal enzymes, so enzymes are secreted rather than trafficked to the lysosome, causing accumulation of inclusion bodies in I-cell disease (ML II).
explanation.whyCorrect: Deficiency of GlcNAc-1-phosphotransferase prevents mannose-6-phosphate tagging of lysosomal enzymes, so enzymes are secreted rather than trafficked to the lysosome, causing accumulation of inclusion bodies in I-cell disease (ML II).

questions.stem: The right image shows a hand assuming carpopedal spasm (wrist/MCP flexion, finger extension, thumb adduction) after the arm is compressed. This clinical sign most strongly indicates which underlying abnormality?

explanation.summary: This is the Trousseau sign: inflating a blood-pressure cuff provokes carpopedal spasm in latent tetany. Low ionized calcium lowers the threshold for nerve depolarization, producing the characteristic spasm.
explanation.whyCorrect: This is the Trousseau sign: inflating a blood-pressure cuff provokes carpopedal spasm in latent tetany. Low ionized calcium lowers the threshold for nerve depolarization, producing the characteristic spasm.

questions.stem: This infant shows short stature, coarse facial features, and a protuberant abdomen (organomegaly). This constellation is most characteristic of which category of inherited disease?

choices.label: C
choices.text: A glycogen debranching defect limited to muscle

explanation.summary: Progressive accumulation of undegraded substrate in lysosomal storage diseases (e.g., mucopolysaccharidoses, I-cell disease) produces coarse features, hepatosplenomegaly with protuberant abdomen, skeletal changes, and developmental delay.
explanation.whyCorrect: Progressive accumulation of undegraded substrate in lysosomal storage diseases (e.g., mucopolysaccharidoses, I-cell disease) produces coarse features, hepatosplenomegaly with protuberant abdomen, skeletal changes, and developmental delay.

questions.stem: The electron micrographs contrast a normal biconcave cell (left) with the deformed crescent-shaped cell (right). The shape change on the right results directly from what molecular event?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Accumulation of excess iron within the cytoplasm

choices.label: B
choices.text: Loss of the cell membrane cytoskeleton due to complement attack

choices.label: C
choices.text: Polymerization of deoxygenated hemoglobin S into rigid fibers

choices.label: D
choices.text: Antibody-mediated agglutination of the red cell surface

choices.label: E
choices.text: Osmotic swelling from impaired sodium pumping

explanation.summary: In sickle cell disease, a valine-for-glutamate substitution in beta-globin causes deoxygenated HbS to polymerize into rigid fibers that distort the red cell into the characteristic sickle/crescent shape seen on the right.
explanation.whyCorrect: In sickle cell disease, a valine-for-glutamate substitution in beta-globin causes deoxygenated HbS to polymerize into rigid fibers that distort the red cell into the characteristic sickle/crescent shape seen on the right.

questions.stem: In this individual, dietary lactose is not cleaved at the intestinal brush border and instead reaches colonic bacteria. What mechanism most directly produces the resulting watery diarrhea?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Bacterial invasion of the intestinal wall causing inflammation

choices.label: C
choices.text: Autoimmune destruction of the intestinal epithelium

choices.label: D
choices.text: Direct toxin-mediated activation of enterocyte chloride secretion

choices.label: E
choices.text: Unabsorbed lactose and its metabolites osmotically draw water into the gut lumen

explanation.summary: Lactase deficiency leaves lactose undigested; bacterial fermentation yields gas and lactic acid, and the osmotically active undigested sugar plus metabolites pull water into the lumen, producing osmotic diarrhea, bloating and flatulence.
explanation.whyCorrect: Lactase deficiency leaves lactose undigested; bacterial fermentation yields gas and lactic acid, and the osmotically active undigested sugar plus metabolites pull water into the lumen, producing osmotic diarrhea, bloating and flatulence.

questions.stem: A child develops hypoglycemia, jaundice, and hepatomegaly after ingesting fruit. The figure illustrates the metabolic step that becomes toxic when a specific enzyme is deficient. Deficiency of which enzyme accounts for the accumulating intermediate that causes this cellular toxicity?

explanation.summary: Fructokinase phosphorylates fructose to fructose 1-phosphate. In hereditary fructose intolerance, aldolase B deficiency prevents further metabolism, so fructose 1-phosphate accumulates and traps phosphate, depleting ATP and causing hypoglycemia, jaundice, and hepatomegaly.
explanation.whyCorrect: Fructokinase phosphorylates fructose to fructose 1-phosphate. In hereditary fructose intolerance, aldolase B deficiency prevents further metabolism, so fructose 1-phosphate accumulates and traps phosphate, depleting ATP and causing hypoglycemia, jaundice, and hepatomegaly.

questions.stem: A patient shows the eyelid deposits, corneal ring, and tendon nodules pictured. These findings together most strongly suggest which underlying disorder?

explanation.summary: Xanthelasma, corneal arcus, and tendon xanthomas (Achilles, extensor tendons) reflect chronic marked LDL cholesterol elevation. In a young patient this constellation is classic for familial hypercholesterolemia (LDL-receptor pathway defect).
explanation.whyCorrect: Xanthelasma, corneal arcus, and tendon xanthomas (Achilles, extensor tendons) reflect chronic marked LDL cholesterol elevation. In a young patient this constellation is classic for familial hypercholesterolemia (LDL-receptor pathway defect).

questions.stem: The figure depicts a reciprocal exchange between chromosomes 9 and 22 that fuses the ABL and BCR regions, producing the derivative small chromosome shown at right. This abnormality is the hallmark of which malignancy?

explanation.summary: The t(9;22) translocation creates the BCR-ABL fusion gene on the shortened chromosome 22 (Philadelphia chromosome). BCR-ABL is a constitutively active tyrosine kinase and is the defining lesion of chronic myeloid leukemia, targeted by imatinib.
explanation.whyCorrect: The t(9;22) translocation creates the BCR-ABL fusion gene on the shortened chromosome 22 (Philadelphia chromosome). BCR-ABL is a constitutively active tyrosine kinase and is the defining lesion of chronic myeloid leukemia, targeted by imatinib.

questions.stem: These clinical photographs show multiple large, flat, uniformly hyperpigmented macules with irregular borders on the trunk of a child. These skin findings, when numerous, are a diagnostic criterion for which condition?

explanation.summary: The lesions are cafe-au-lait macules. Six or more measuring >5 mm (prepubertal) are a diagnostic criterion for neurofibromatosis type 1, a neurocutaneous disorder caused by mutation of the NF1 tumor-suppressor gene (neurofibromin).
explanation.whyCorrect: The lesions are cafe-au-lait macules. Six or more measuring >5 mm (prepubertal) are a diagnostic criterion for neurofibromatosis type 1, a neurocutaneous disorder caused by mutation of the NF1 tumor-suppressor gene (neurofibromin).

questions.stem: This patient has velvety, hyperpigmented, thickened skin in the posterior neck folds. What condition is shown and what does it most commonly signal?

choices.label: C
choices.text: Acanthosis nigricans, associated with insulin resistance

choices.label: E
choices.text: Tinea versicolor, a superficial fungal infection

explanation.summary: Acanthosis nigricans presents as symmetric velvety hyperpigmentation in flexural areas such as the neck and axillae. It most often reflects hyperinsulinemia/insulin resistance (obesity, type 2 diabetes) and can occasionally be a paraneoplastic sign.
explanation.whyCorrect: Acanthosis nigricans presents as symmetric velvety hyperpigmentation in flexural areas such as the neck and axillae. It most often reflects hyperinsulinemia/insulin resistance (obesity, type 2 diabetes) and can occasionally be a paraneoplastic sign.

questions.stem: This pediatric radiograph shows bowing of the long bones and widened, poorly mineralized metaphyses. What deficiency-related condition does it depict?

explanation.summary: Bowing deformities and cupped, frayed metaphyses in a child indicate rickets, caused by vitamin D (or calcium/phosphate) deficiency leading to defective mineralization of growing bone.
explanation.whyCorrect: Bowing deformities and cupped, frayed metaphyses in a child indicate rickets, caused by vitamin D (or calcium/phosphate) deficiency leading to defective mineralization of growing bone.

questions.stem: This eye shows a brownish ring of pigment at the corneal limbus. It is most characteristic of which systemic disorder?

explanation.summary: The ring is a Kayser-Fleischer ring, caused by copper deposition in Descemet membrane. It is a hallmark of Wilson disease, in which defective biliary copper excretion leads to copper accumulation in the cornea, liver, and brain.
explanation.whyCorrect: The ring is a Kayser-Fleischer ring, caused by copper deposition in Descemet membrane. It is a hallmark of Wilson disease, in which defective biliary copper excretion leads to copper accumulation in the cornea, liver, and brain.

questions.stem: This toddler presents with a well-demarcated erythematous, scaling rash around the mouth and other periorificial areas. Deficiency of which nutrient most classically produces this pattern?

explanation.summary: Periorificial and acral dermatitis, as seen here, is the hallmark of zinc deficiency (acrodermatitis enteropathica), which also causes alopecia and diarrhea. Zinc is a cofactor for numerous metalloenzymes, and rapidly dividing epithelium is highly sensitive to its lack.
explanation.whyCorrect: Periorificial and acral dermatitis, as seen here, is the hallmark of zinc deficiency (acrodermatitis enteropathica), which also causes alopecia and diarrhea. Zinc is a cofactor for numerous metalloenzymes, and rapidly dividing epithelium is highly sensitive to its lack.

questions.stem: This patient shows bluish-black pigment deposition in the sclera and periocular tissue. In a patient with a tyrosine-catabolism enzyme deficiency and urine that darkens on standing, this finding is termed what?

explanation.summary: Ochronosis is deposition of polymerized homogentisic acid pigment in connective tissue and sclera in alkaptonuria (homogentisate oxidase deficiency).
explanation.whyCorrect: Ochronosis is deposition of polymerized homogentisic acid pigment in connective tissue and sclera in alkaptonuria (homogentisate oxidase deficiency).

questions.stem: This intraoperative image shows a strikingly dark, greenish-black liver. This appearance is characteristic of which inherited hyperbilirubinemia?

explanation.summary: Dubin-Johnson syndrome (defective MRP2/canalicular transport of conjugated bilirubin) causes accumulation of dark pigment giving the liver a grossly black appearance, with conjugated hyperbilirubinemia.
explanation.whyCorrect: Dubin-Johnson syndrome (defective MRP2/canalicular transport of conjugated bilirubin) causes accumulation of dark pigment giving the liver a grossly black appearance, with conjugated hyperbilirubinemia.

questions.stem: A middle-aged patient with new-onset diabetes and cirrhosis develops this diffuse bronze-brown skin discoloration. Which disorder best explains this presentation?

explanation.summary: Iron overload from hemochromatosis deposits in skin and stimulates melanin production, producing the classic bronze/slate-gray hyperpigmentation. The combination of skin pigmentation, diabetes, and cirrhosis is the classic 'bronze diabetes' triad.
explanation.whyCorrect: Iron overload from hemochromatosis deposits in skin and stimulates melanin production, producing the classic bronze/slate-gray hyperpigmentation. The combination of skin pigmentation, diabetes, and cirrhosis is the classic 'bronze diabetes' triad.

questions.stem: This pathway highlights a block at porphobilinogen deaminase, causing accumulation of ALA and porphobilinogen. What is the classic clinical presentation of the disorder produced by this enzyme deficiency?

choices.label: D
choices.text: Acute neurovisceral attacks with abdominal pain and neuropsychiatric symptoms

explanation.summary: Deficiency of porphobilinogen deaminase causes acute intermittent porphyria. Buildup of ALA and porphobilinogen produces episodic abdominal pain, neuropathy, and neuropsychiatric changes, typically without photosensitivity since no photosensitizing porphyrins accumulate.
explanation.whyCorrect: Deficiency of porphobilinogen deaminase causes acute intermittent porphyria. Buildup of ALA and porphobilinogen produces episodic abdominal pain, neuropathy, and neuropsychiatric changes, typically without photosensitivity since no photosensitizing porphyrins accumulate.

questions.stem: This inset marks a deficiency of uroporphyrinogen decarboxylase, the defect in the most common porphyria. How does this disorder typically present clinically?

choices.label: B
choices.text: Recurrent acute abdominal pain attacks without skin findings

choices.label: C
choices.text: Photosensitive blistering and fragility of sun-exposed skin

choices.label: D
choices.text: Hepatic cirrhosis with no cutaneous signs

explanation.summary: Uroporphyrinogen decarboxylase deficiency causes porphyria cutanea tarda, the most common porphyria. Accumulated photosensitizing uroporphyrins deposit in skin, producing blistering, fragility, and hyperpigmentation on sun-exposed areas; it is often associated with liver disease.
explanation.whyCorrect: Uroporphyrinogen decarboxylase deficiency causes porphyria cutanea tarda, the most common porphyria. Accumulated photosensitizing uroporphyrins deposit in skin, producing blistering, fragility, and hyperpigmentation on sun-exposed areas; it is often associated with liver disease.

questions.stem: These vesicles, erosions, hemorrhagic crusts, and scarring on the sun-exposed dorsal hands are characteristic of which condition?

explanation.summary: Porphyria cutanea tarda causes skin fragility and blistering on sun-exposed skin (especially the dorsal hands) due to photosensitizing uroporphyrins from uroporphyrinogen decarboxylase deficiency. Lesions heal with scarring, milia, and hyperpigmentation.
explanation.whyCorrect: Porphyria cutanea tarda causes skin fragility and blistering on sun-exposed skin (especially the dorsal hands) due to photosensitizing uroporphyrins from uroporphyrinogen decarboxylase deficiency. Lesions heal with scarring, milia, and hyperpigmentation.

questions.stem: This sequence illustrates how a lipoprotein drives early plaque formation in an artery wall. Why do macrophages engulf so much of the modified lipoprotein that they become foam cells?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Native LDL receptors are upregulated by intracellular cholesterol

choices.label: B
choices.text: Insulin receptors stimulate cholesterol esterification in macrophages

choices.label: C
choices.text: HDL receptors actively pump cholesterol into the macrophage

choices.label: D
choices.text: Scavenger receptors take up oxidized LDL without feedback downregulation, allowing unchecked lipid accumulation

choices.label: E
choices.text: Chylomicron remnant receptors bind oxidized LDL in the intima

explanation.summary: After endothelial injury, monocytes enter the intima and become macrophages that take up oxidized LDL through scavenger receptors. Unlike the classic LDL receptor, scavenger receptors are not suppressed by rising intracellular cholesterol, so lipid loading continues until the cells become foam cells that release cytokines and recruit smooth muscle cells, forming the fatty streak.
explanation.whyCorrect: After endothelial injury, monocytes enter the intima and become macrophages that take up oxidized LDL through scavenger receptors. Unlike the classic LDL receptor, scavenger receptors are not suppressed by rising intracellular cholesterol, so lipid loading continues until the cells become foam cells that release cytokines and recruit smooth muscle cells, forming the fatty streak.

questions.stem: The arrow points to a gray-white ring at the peripheral cornea in this eye. In a young or middle-aged patient, this finding is most strongly associated with which condition?

explanation.summary: The finding is corneal arcus, a ring of lipid deposition at the corneal margin. In older patients it is an age-related change (arcus senilis), but when it appears before roughly age 45 it suggests a significant lipid disorder such as familial hypercholesterolemia. (A copper-colored Kayser-Fleischer ring, by contrast, indicates Wilson disease.)
explanation.whyCorrect: The finding is corneal arcus, a ring of lipid deposition at the corneal margin. In older patients it is an age-related change (arcus senilis), but when it appears before roughly age 45 it suggests a significant lipid disorder such as familial hypercholesterolemia. (A copper-colored Kayser-Fleischer ring, by contrast, indicates Wilson disease.)

questions.stem: This skin shows crops of small yellow-red papules with erythematous bases. These lesions are most characteristic of which underlying metabolic abnormality?

choices.label: D
choices.text: Vitamin C deficiency with perifollicular hemorrhage

explanation.summary: These are eruptive xanthomas, sudden crops of lipid-laden papules that arise when serum triglycerides are markedly elevated (as in familial hyperchylomicronemia or severe combined hyperlipidemia). They are composed of triglyceride-laden macrophages and typically regress when triglyceride levels are lowered, distinguishing them from the tendon xanthomas of hypercholesterolemia.
explanation.whyCorrect: These are eruptive xanthomas, sudden crops of lipid-laden papules that arise when serum triglycerides are markedly elevated (as in familial hyperchylomicronemia or severe combined hyperlipidemia). They are composed of triglyceride-laden macrophages and typically regress when triglyceride levels are lowered, distinguishing them from the tendon xanthomas of hypercholesterolemia.

questions.stem: The large central cell shows a characteristic 'crumpled tissue paper' cytoplasm. Deficiency of which enzyme produces this cell?

explanation.summary: This is a Gaucher cell, a lipid-laden macrophage seen in Gaucher disease. Deficiency of glucocerebrosidase causes accumulation of glucocerebroside, giving the cytoplasm its wrinkled 'tissue paper' appearance; features include hepatosplenomegaly, anemia, thrombocytopenia, and bone disease.
explanation.whyCorrect: This is a Gaucher cell, a lipid-laden macrophage seen in Gaucher disease. Deficiency of glucocerebrosidase causes accumulation of glucocerebroside, giving the cytoplasm its wrinkled 'tissue paper' appearance; features include hepatosplenomegaly, anemia, thrombocytopenia, and bone disease.

questions.stem: The arrow points to three copies of chromosome 21 in this karyotype. What clinical syndrome does this abnormality produce?

explanation.summary: Three copies of chromosome 21 (trisomy 21) cause Down syndrome, characterized by intellectual disability, characteristic facies, and increased risk of congenital heart defects. Edwards (18) and Patau (13) are other autosomal trisomies.
explanation.whyCorrect: Three copies of chromosome 21 (trisomy 21) cause Down syndrome, characterized by intellectual disability, characteristic facies, and increased risk of congenital heart defects. Edwards (18) and Patau (13) are other autosomal trisomies.

questions.stem: A phenotypically normal person is found to be a balanced carrier of the chromosomal rearrangement fusing chromosomes 14 and 21 shown here. This carrier is at increased risk of having a child with which condition?

explanation.summary: A rob(14;21) Robertsonian translocation carrier is balanced and healthy, but during gamete formation can produce unbalanced gametes carrying the extra chromosome 21 material, leading to translocation Down syndrome in offspring.
explanation.whyCorrect: A rob(14;21) Robertsonian translocation carrier is balanced and healthy, but during gamete formation can produce unbalanced gametes carrying the extra chromosome 21 material, leading to translocation Down syndrome in offspring.

questions.stem: The upper structural chromosome abnormality depicted (loss of the terminal short arm of chromosome 5) produces which classic clinical syndrome?

explanation.summary: Terminal deletion of the short arm of chromosome 5 (5p-) causes cri-du-chat syndrome, characterized by a high-pitched cat-like cry, microcephaly, and intellectual disability.
explanation.whyCorrect: Terminal deletion of the short arm of chromosome 5 (5p-) causes cri-du-chat syndrome, characterized by a high-pitched cat-like cry, microcephaly, and intellectual disability.

questions.stem: A neonate shows the clenched fists with overlapping fingers, rocker-bottom/clubfeet, low-set ears, and cleft lip depicted here. The underlying extra whole chromosome most commonly arises by which mechanism?

explanation.summary: These findings are those of trisomy 18 (Edwards syndrome); like most autosomal trisomies it usually results from maternal meiotic nondisjunction producing an extra copy of the chromosome.
explanation.whyCorrect: These findings are those of trisomy 18 (Edwards syndrome); like most autosomal trisomies it usually results from maternal meiotic nondisjunction producing an extra copy of the chromosome.

questions.stem: A short-statured adolescent girl shows the increased elbow carrying angle (cubitus valgus) demonstrated on the right. These features are characteristic of which chromosomal condition?

explanation.summary: Short stature and cubitus valgus (wide carrying angle), along with webbed neck and gonadal dysgenesis, are hallmarks of Turner syndrome (45,X).
explanation.whyCorrect: Short stature and cubitus valgus (wide carrying angle), along with webbed neck and gonadal dysgenesis, are hallmarks of Turner syndrome (45,X).

questions.stem: A phenotypically male patient shows the tall stature, gynecomastia, sparse body hair, female-type pubic hair pattern, small testes, and micropenis illustrated here. Which chromosomal complement best explains this presentation?

explanation.summary: The combination of eunuchoid tall stature, gynecomastia, small firm testes, and hypoandrogenic features is classic for Klinefelter syndrome (47,XXY), in which an extra X chromosome causes primary hypogonadism and testosterone deficiency.
explanation.whyCorrect: The combination of eunuchoid tall stature, gynecomastia, small firm testes, and hypoandrogenic features is classic for Klinefelter syndrome (47,XXY), in which an extra X chromosome causes primary hypogonadism and testosterone deficiency.

questions.stem: The microdeletion marked on the long arm of chromosome 22 in this figure is most classically associated with which clinical syndrome?

explanation.summary: A hemizygous deletion at 22q11.2 causes DiGeorge/velocardiofacial syndrome, featuring conotruncal cardiac defects, thymic and parathyroid hypoplasia, palatal anomalies, and characteristic facies.
explanation.whyCorrect: A hemizygous deletion at 22q11.2 causes DiGeorge/velocardiofacial syndrome, featuring conotruncal cardiac defects, thymic and parathyroid hypoplasia, palatal anomalies, and characteristic facies.

questions.stem: In this karyotype, red arrows highlight abnormal-length chromosomes 9 and 22, with material exchanged between the two. What abnormality is depicted and with which malignancy is it classically associated?

choices.label: A
choices.text: A 22q11 microdeletion, seen in DiGeorge syndrome

choices.label: B
choices.text: Monosomy X, seen in Turner syndrome

choices.label: C
choices.text: A chromosome 5p deletion, seen in cri-du-chat syndrome

choices.label: D
choices.text: A reciprocal t(9;22) translocation (Philadelphia chromosome), seen in chronic myeloid leukemia

explanation.summary: The arrows mark a shortened 22 (derivative 22) and altered 9 from the reciprocal t(9;22) translocation. This Philadelphia chromosome creates the BCR-ABL1 fusion characteristic of chronic myeloid leukemia.
explanation.whyCorrect: The arrows mark a shortened 22 (derivative 22) and altered 9 from the reciprocal t(9;22) translocation. This Philadelphia chromosome creates the BCR-ABL1 fusion characteristic of chronic myeloid leukemia.

questions.stem: An infant presents with the constellation of findings shown: mousy body odor, microcephaly, developmental delay, dry skin/eczema, and elevated serum phenylalanine. What is the most likely diagnosis?

explanation.summary: Phenylalanine hydroxylase deficiency causes phenylalanine accumulation, producing the classic mousy/musty odor, intellectual disability, microcephaly, seizures, eczema, and hypopigmentation. Management is dietary phenylalanine restriction.
explanation.whyCorrect: Phenylalanine hydroxylase deficiency causes phenylalanine accumulation, producing the classic mousy/musty odor, intellectual disability, microcephaly, seizures, eczema, and hypopigmentation. Management is dietary phenylalanine restriction.

questions.stem: This hand shows diffuse erythema, warmth, and swelling spreading across the dorsum and digits, with poorly demarcated borders. What is the most likely clinical diagnosis?

explanation.summary: Cellulitis is a spreading bacterial infection of the dermis and subcutaneous tissue producing warm, tender, erythematous, poorly demarcated swelling. It is distinguished from vascular conditions like Raynaud or cyanosis, which cause color change rather than warm inflammatory swelling.
explanation.whyCorrect: Cellulitis is a spreading bacterial infection of the dermis and subcutaneous tissue producing warm, tender, erythematous, poorly demarcated swelling. It is distinguished from vascular conditions like Raynaud or cyanosis, which cause color change rather than warm inflammatory swelling.

questions.stem: This hand shows bluish discoloration confined to the distal fingers while the palm remains pink. What does this finding represent?

explanation.summary: Peripheral (acral) cyanosis reflects increased oxygen extraction and sluggish flow in the extremities, giving a bluish tint to the fingers/nail beds while mucous membranes stay pink. Central cyanosis, by contrast, involves the tongue/lips and reflects arterial desaturation.
explanation.whyCorrect: Peripheral (acral) cyanosis reflects increased oxygen extraction and sluggish flow in the extremities, giving a bluish tint to the fingers/nail beds while mucous membranes stay pink. Central cyanosis, by contrast, involves the tongue/lips and reflects arterial desaturation.
